EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought ice, sleet and extreme cold to southeast Texas from the 24th through the 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm moved into southeast Texas on the night of the 24th. Precipitation began on the night of the 24th, when the College Station ASOS station KCLL measured a trace of Freezing Rain. Sleet occurred overnight with a trained storm spotter measuring around 0.1 inches of sleet early on the morning of the 24th. Not long after, a local emergency manager reported very slick conditions across College Station and Bryan, including along Highway 6 and OSR. Later that morning, a trained storm spotter shared a photo on social media showing ice accumulations. In the photo, around 0.25 inches of flat ice and 0.125 inches of radial ice was measured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought ice, sleet and extreme cold to southeast Texas from the 24th through the 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm moved into southeast Texas on the night of the 24th. After the storm cleared the next day, Trinity county Sheriffs Office reported that bridges had been iced over across the county. One person had also reportedly slid off the road as a result.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong arctic cold front moved across west Texas and southeast New Mexico during the afternoon and evening hours of the 23rd. Meanwhile, a potent upper-level trough neared the region from the west. Widespread rain began during the morning of the 23rd in western portions of the area and gradually transitioned eastward through the day. As the front moved across the area, rain transitioned to sleet and snow from north to south (with freezing rain being more common south of the Pecos River and in the Davis Mountains). Off and on bands of sleet and snow continued across the Permian Basin and southeast New Mexico on the 24th, and became mostly snow by the 25th before moving east of the area late in the afternoon. Snowfall/sleet totals reached winter storm criteria across much of the northern basin and in southeast New Mexico. Lower snow and ice totals were reported further south.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public reported 0.1 inch of ice due to heavy freezing rain. Thunder was reported and GLM satellite imagery showed cloud-to-cloud lightning during the time of the report. The total duration of the winter weather event is estimated from AWOS data and radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: All areas of far west Texas were put into Moderate(D1) drought or better by the end of January.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The month started out with the eastern portion of the zone in Severe(D2) drought with Moderate(D1) drought over the far west.  On Jan 27th, the entire zone was in Moderate(D1) drought.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: All areas of far west Texas were put into Moderate(D1) drought or better by the end of January.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The far eastern portion of the zone started out in Severe(D2) drought with Moderate(D1) drought elsewhere.  On January 27th, the entire zone was in Moderate(D1) or Abnormally Dry(D0) drought conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Across South Texas and the Coastal Bend, persistent dry conditions through late January led to worsening drought and critically low soil moisture, limiting agricultural progress and stressing rangeland. Periods of unseasonably warm weather were followed by hard freezes and a winter storm, though precipitation was generally limited, with only modest rainfall of up to around an inch in parts of the Coastal Bend providing minor short-term improvement to soil moisture. Producers largely completed field preparation but delayed spring planting due to insufficient moisture for germination. Winter pastures and forage growth struggled under the dry conditions, resulting in poor to fair range conditions and increased reliance on hay and supplemental feed for livestock. While citrus and cool-season vegetable harvests continued, drought stress remained a concern for crop development and pasture recovery, and additional rainfall will be needed to improve soil moisture and support the upcoming planting season.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Continued D2 and D3 drought. D3 expanded to cover whole county on 1/13.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The I-35 Corridor, Rio Grande and Coastal Plains received less than normal precipitation during the month, and the drought worsened or remained unchanged. Caldwell, Fayette, Guadalupe, Hays, Lavaca, Maverick, Travis, and Zavala Counties went from Severe (D2) Drought category to Extreme (D3) Drought. Williamson County went from Moderate (D1) Drought to D2. Atascosa, Bastrop, Bexar, Comal, De Witt, Dimmit, Frio, Gonzales, Karnes, Lee, Medina, and Wilson Counties remained in D3. Blanco, Kendall, and Kinney remained in D2. The Edwards Aquifer level was unchanged but was still 39.1 feet below normal. Area reservoirs were below normal conservation levels. Lake Travis dropped 0.5 feet and was 13.8 feet below normal. Canyon Lake dropped 0.6 feet and was 21.0 feet below normal. The 7-day average streamflow was much below (<10%) normal on the Lavaca, Blanco, San Marcos, lower Guadalupe, Medina, lower Nueces, and Atascosa Rivers, and below (10%-24%) normal on the San Gabriel, Colorado, upper Guadalupe, San Antonio, and Frio Rivers. Most water providers encouraged conservation while San Antonio and Universal City imposed stage 3 restrictions and New Braunfels and Austin had stage 2. Of the counties in D2 or worse drought, Atascosa, Bastrop, Blanco, Bexar, Caldwell, Comal, De Witt, Dimmit, Fayette, Frio, Guadalupe, Hays, Karnes, Medina, Wilson, and Zavala had outdoor burn bans in effect at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A classic winter weather pattern setup across the region with an upper low moving through northwest Mexico while a back door cold front moved into Hudspeth County. Southwest flow aloft ahead of the upper low kept some warm air aloft over the area and the eastern Hudspeth County experienced a period of freezing rain with over a half inch reported around Dell City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Rio Grande Electric Cooperative reported power outages around Dell City related to the ice storm.  Reports of up to 1 inch radius ice was on powerlines causing these outages.

As the front moved across the area, rain transitioned to sleet and snow from north to south (with freezing rain being more common south of the Pecos River and in the Davis Mountains). Off and on bands of sleet and snow continued across the Permian Basin and southeast New Mexico on the 24th, and became mostly snow by the 25th before moving east of the area late in the afternoon. Snowfall/sleet totals reached winter storm criteria across much of the northern basin and in southeast New Mexico. Lower snow and ice totals were reported further south.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Odessa Fire/Rescue reported 2.2 inches of sleet/snow via Slack during the morning of the 25th. The total duration of the event is estimated from radar and nearby ASOS reports.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Across South Texas and the Coastal Bend, persistent dry conditions through late January led to worsening drought and critically low soil moisture, limiting agricultural progress and stressing rangeland. Periods of unseasonably warm weather were followed by hard freezes and a winter storm, though precipitation was generally limited, with only modest rainfall of up to around an inch in parts of the Coastal Bend providing minor short-term improvement to soil moisture. Producers largely completed field preparation but delayed spring planting due to insufficient moisture for germination. Winter pastures and forage growth struggled under the dry conditions, resulting in poor to fair range conditions and increased reliance on hay and supplemental feed for livestock. While citrus and cool-season vegetable harvests continued, drought stress remained a concern for crop development and pasture recovery, and additional rainfall will be needed to improve soil moisture and support the upcoming planting season.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Continued D2 and D3 drought.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The I-35 Corridor, Rio Grande and Coastal Plains received less than normal precipitation during the month, and the drought worsened or remained unchanged. Caldwell, Fayette, Guadalupe, Hays, Lavaca, Maverick, Travis, and Zavala Counties went from Severe (D2) Drought category to Extreme (D3) Drought. Williamson County went from Moderate (D1) Drought to D2. Atascosa, Bastrop, Bexar, Comal, De Witt, Dimmit, Frio, Gonzales, Karnes, Lee, Medina, and Wilson Counties remained in D3. Blanco, Kendall, and Kinney remained in D2. The Edwards Aquifer level was unchanged but was still 39.1 feet below normal. Area reservoirs were below normal conservation levels. Lake Travis dropped 0.5 feet and was 13.8 feet below normal. Canyon Lake dropped 0.6 feet and was 21.0 feet below normal. The 7-day average streamflow was much below (<10%) normal on the Lavaca, Blanco, San Marcos, lower Guadalupe, Medina, lower Nueces, and Atascosa Rivers, and below (10%-24%) normal on the San Gabriel, Colorado, upper Guadalupe, San Antonio, and Frio Rivers. Most water providers encouraged conservation while San Antonio and Universal City imposed stage 3 restrictions and New Braunfels and Austin had stage 2. Of the counties in D2 or worse drought, Atascosa, Bastrop, Blanco, Bexar, Caldwell, Comal, De Witt, Dimmit, Fayette, Frio, Guadalupe, Hays, Karnes, Medina, Wilson, and Zavala had outdoor burn bans in effect at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought ice, sleet and extreme cold to southeast Texas from the 24th through the 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm moved into southeast Texas on the night of the 24th. Late in the night, an amateur radio operator reported sleet on TX 21 between Crockett and Austonio. On the morning of the 25th, Texas Parks & Wildlife Department measured 0.25 inches of ice in Mission Tejas State Park. Houston county Sheriffs Office later reported multiple power outages and downed tree limbs across the county in the aftermath of the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought ice, sleet and extreme cold to southeast Texas from the 24th through the 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm moved into southeast Texas on the night of the 24th. A mPING report from the public was received during the early morning of the 25th, indicating that freezing rain was occurring just east of Madisonville. Later that morning, Madison county Sheriffs Office reported a few downed trees and limbs with slick roads across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought ice, sleet and extreme cold to southeast Texas from the 24th through the 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm moved into southeast Texas on the night of the 24th. A storm chaser shared a video on social media of sleet falling in Conroe late that night. During the early morning hours of the 25th, the ASOS station at Conroe-North Houston Regional Airport KCXO reported freezing rain. Not long after, a member of the public shared pictures on social media, showing sleet beginning to accumulate in Shenandoah (southern Montgomery County). A NWS Employee also reported that streets and highways in the area were mostly wet during the pre-dawn hours, but some parts of the road had ice glazed over them. This employee also reported around 0.20 inches of sleet. A few hours later, local law enforcement reported ice on I-45 North at SH 242. An emergency manager reported 0.06 inches of ice near Patton Village. In the aftermath of the winter precipitation, Texas department of transportation (TXDOT) reported closures on Woodlands Parkway flyover (Woodlands Parkway and I-45), for both the northbound and southbound lanes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought ice, sleet and extreme cold to southeast Texas from the 24th through the 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm moves into southeast Texas late on the night of the 24th. The next morning after precipitation ended, an emergency manager reported 0.10 inches of freezing rain in Polk county. A few power outages were also reported in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southwesterly downsloping winds yielded a brief period of high winds at the KC Ranch Mesonet Station.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The West Texas Mesonet station at KC Ranch 14 W Toyavale recorded intermittent high wind gusts, including a gust up to 64 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the morning of the 8th, a shortwave and upper-level jet max began to move over west Texas and southeast New Mexico while a surface cyclone strengthened over the Oklahoma Panhandle. As the jet max moved overhead, very strong to high winds developed in the higher terrain (namely the Guadalupe and Davis Mountains), as well as the surrounding foothills. Gusts up to 80-90 mph were observed in the Guadalupe Mountains and at Carlsbad Caverns National Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The West Texas Mesonet station 60 Pine Springs GMNP measured numerous high wind gusts, including an 80 mph gust.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mesonet station PSGT2 Pinery measured numerous high wind gusts, including a 63 mph gust. The total time of occurrence is partially estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Across South Texas and the Coastal Bend, persistent dry conditions through late January led to worsening drought and critically low soil moisture, limiting agricultural progress and stressing rangeland. Periods of unseasonably warm weather were followed by hard freezes and a winter storm, though precipitation was generally limited, with only modest rainfall of up to around an inch in parts of the Coastal Bend providing minor short-term improvement to soil moisture. Producers largely completed field preparation but delayed spring planting due to insufficient moisture for germination. Winter pastures and forage growth struggled under the dry conditions, resulting in poor to fair range conditions and increased reliance on hay and supplemental feed for livestock. While citrus and cool-season vegetable harvests continued, drought stress remained a concern for crop development and pasture recovery, and additional rainfall will be needed to improve soil moisture and support the upcoming planting season.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Introduction of D2 drought.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong arctic cold front moved across west Texas and southeast New Mexico during the afternoon and evening hours of the 23rd. Meanwhile, a potent upper-level trough neared the region from the west. Widespread rain began during the morning of the 23rd in western portions of the area and gradually transitioned eastward through the day. As the front moved across the area, rain transitioned to sleet and snow from north to south (with freezing rain being more common south of the Pecos River and in the Davis Mountains). Off and on bands of sleet and snow continued across the Permian Basin and southeast New Mexico on the 24th, and became mostly snow by the 25th before moving east of the area late in the afternoon. Snowfall/sleet totals reached winter storm criteria across much of the northern basin and in southeast New Mexico. Lower snow and ice totals were reported further south.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public reported a two day sleet/snow total of 3 inches of snow and 1 inch of sleet. The report was received via social media. The total duration of the event is estimated from AWOS data and radar.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: KVCT reported temperatures of 32 degF or less for a least 2 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Little to no rainfall and a mixture of heat followed by cold and generally dry weather, including a hard freeze toward the end of January combined to steadily worsen drought conditions across northern Hidalgo, Jim Hogg, Brooks, Starr, and Zapata County.  Coverage of Extreme Drought increased to more than 70% of these areas by the 13th, which continued through month's end.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: By mid month, Severe (D2) to Extreme (D3) Drought conditions expanded westward to encompass much of Zapata County with the exception of the extreme west-northwestern part of the county, which remained under D1 (Moderate) Drought. D3 Drought was observed over the eastern half of Zapata County. These conditions remained in place through the remainder of the month.||Ranchers began to report difficulty maintaining cattle herds and needing to haul water to the ranch to sustain them.  Hay availability was becoming limited.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper-level low moved across northern Mexico bringing a strong cold front through South-Central Texas. This resulted in very cold air in the boundary layer with a layer of above freezing air aloft which produced sleet and freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mix of sleet and freezing rain began during the morning of the 24th. DriveTexas.org reported that RR 337 was closed due to icing near Leakey that evening. The morning of the 25th a CoCoRaHS observer reported 0.5 of sleet in Leakey.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper-level low moved across northern Mexico bringing a strong cold front through South-Central Texas. This resulted in very cold air in the boundary layer with a layer of above freezing air aloft which produced sleet and freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mix of sleet and freezing rain began during the morning of the 24th. TXDoT reported the main lanes of Toll 183A closed due to icing at around 7 pm. Storm total accumulations included 1.1 of sleet in Round Rock, 1 of sleet in Liberty Hill, 1 of sleet in Cedar Park, and 1.5 of sleet in Hutto.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The passage of a very subtle reinforcing front and high pressure settling over the region and gradually progressing eastward through the night, locked in light, variable, but mostly offshore winds for a cool, dry overnight into Sunday morning. This set us up for a chilly night, with light freezes experienced across much of the northern CWA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: KNQI reported temperatures of 32 degF or less for a least 2 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of winter weather affected South Texas during the final week of January 2026, beginning with the arrival of a strong Arctic front early on January 24. The front surged southward faster than initially anticipated, producing a rapid temperature drop across the region and widespread rain behind the boundary. As colder air deepened overnight, precipitation continued while temperatures approached or fell below freezing across parts of the region, creating a narrow window for freezing rain and drizzle late January 24 into the morning of January 25. The greatest risk for ice accumulation occurred across the Victoria Crossroads, where light ice totals of up to around a tenth of an inch were possible. Elsewhere, the combination of lingering precipitation and wet roadways raised concerns for patchy black ice, particularly on bridges and overpasses as temperatures briefly dipped to freezing.||Once the precipitation ended on January 25, the primary hazard transitioned to extreme cold as a deep Arctic air mass settled over South Texas. Temperatures struggled to rise above the 40s during the day, while overnight lows dropped into the teens across northern inland areas and the lower to mid-20s closer to the coast. Persistent northerly winds produced dangerously low wind chills, with values falling into the single digits in some inland locations and the teens elsewhere. Extreme Cold Warnings and Freeze Warnings were issued across the region as the prolonged cold posed risks to infrastructure, agriculture, and vulnerable populations. Clearer skies and lighter winds early in the week allowed efficient radiational cooling, reinforcing the cold conditions through January 27 before gradual moderation began.||Although temperatures slowly recovered into the 50s and 60s by midweek, the pattern remained active with additional frontal passages. A brief warmup ahead of another cold front late in the week pushed temperatures into the 70s before colder air returned by January 30. This secondary surge brought another round of below-freezing temperatures and widespread cold weather headlines, particularly across inland counties where lows fell into the 20s and wind chills dropped into the teens. While precipitation with this second front remained limited, the extended period of repeated freezes and persistent cold made this late-January event a notable winter episode for South Texas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The AWOS 8T6 in George West / Live Oak reported temperatures of 32 degF or less for a least 2 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The I-35 Corridor, Rio Grande and Coastal Plains received less than normal precipitation during the month, and the drought worsened or remained unchanged. Caldwell, Fayette, Guadalupe, Hays, Lavaca, Maverick, Travis, and Zavala Counties went from Severe (D2) Drought category to Extreme (D3) Drought. Williamson County went from Moderate (D1) Drought to D2. Atascosa, Bastrop, Bexar, Comal, De Witt, Dimmit, Frio, Gonzales, Karnes, Lee, Medina, and Wilson Counties remained in D3. Blanco, Kendall, and Kinney remained in D2. The Edwards Aquifer level was unchanged but was still 39.1 feet below normal. Area reservoirs were below normal conservation levels. Lake Travis dropped 0.5 feet and was 13.8 feet below normal. Canyon Lake dropped 0.6 feet and was 21.0 feet below normal. The 7-day average streamflow was much below (<10%) normal on the Lavaca, Blanco, San Marcos, lower Guadalupe, Medina, lower Nueces, and Atascosa Rivers, and below (10%-24%) normal on the San Gabriel, Colorado, upper Guadalupe, San Antonio, and Frio Rivers. Most water providers encouraged conservation while San Antonio and Universal City imposed stage 3 restrictions and New Braunfels and Austin had stage 2. Of the counties in D2 or worse drought, Atascosa, Bastrop, Blanco, Bexar, Caldwell, Comal, De Witt, Dimmit, Fayette, Frio, Guadalupe, Hays, Karnes, Medina, Wilson, and Zavala had outdoor burn bans in effect at the end of the month.

A winter storm brought ice, sleet and extreme cold to southeast Texas from the 24th through the 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm moved into southeast Texas on the night of the 24th. Broadcast media reported sleet falling in Somerville on the night of the 24th. The next day, broadcast media measured 0.21 inches of ice on a small branch in Frenstat in Burleson County after precipitation had ended.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong arctic cold front moved across west Texas and southeast New Mexico during the afternoon and evening hours of the 23rd. Meanwhile, a potent upper-level trough neared the region from the west. Widespread rain began during the morning of the 23rd in western portions of the area and gradually transitioned eastward through the day. As the front moved across the area, rain transitioned to sleet and snow from north to south (with freezing rain being more common south of the Pecos River and in the Davis Mountains). Off and on bands of sleet and snow continued across the Permian Basin and southeast New Mexico on the 24th, and became mostly snow by the 25th before moving east of the area late in the afternoon. Snowfall/sleet totals reached winter storm criteria across much of the northern basin and in southeast New Mexico. Lower snow and ice totals were reported further south.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public reported 4.5 inches of storm total snow/sleet. The total duration of the event is estimated from radar and nearby AWOS reports.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A widespread winter storm affected the region from the 23rd and lasting until the afternoon of the 25th. Beginning on the 23rd, an arctic cold front moved through the region during the morning and afternoon. Shortly after the cold air moved into the region, Pacific moisture began to stream over from the southwest resulting in wintry showers. Precipitation initially began as freezing rain at many locations but quickly switched to sleet as the lower levels of the atmosphere cooled. Despite temperatures well below freezing into the single digits by the morning of the 24th, sleet continued to be the dominant precipitation type. A strong warm layer aloft allowed for much of this precipitation to remain as sleet. By the afternoon of the 24th, the warm layer aloft cooled enough to allow mostly snow to fall as an upper level disturbance approached the region. Snowfall continued through the night of the 24th ending late in the afternoon on the 25th. The heaviest amount of snow fell over the western South Plains into the extreme southern Texas Panhandle where totals of six to eight inches were observed. The remainder of the South Plains and Rolling Plains saw between three and six inches of snowfall. Additionally, breezy winds combined with bitterly cold temperatures producing dangerous wind chills. Observed wind chills were as low as -15 to -20 degrees Fahrenheit.||Snowfall and sleet accumulation totals from National Weather Service cooperative weather observers are listed below:||8.2 inches at A winter storm brought ice, sleet and extreme cold to southeast Texas from the 24th through the 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two people were found dead under a bridge near the 1900 block of Congress. Police reported that the victims appeared to be homeless and likely died as a result of Extreme Cold. The low temperature in Houston this morning was 24 degrees with the windchill reaching as low as 10 degrees. At the time the victims were found, the temperature was 37 degrees with a windchill of 33 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought sleet/snow to much of west central Texas. Some freezing rain resulted in a light glaze of ice across the Northwest Hill Country. The snow/sleet amounts ranged from 1 to 4 inches across the northern 2/3 of the area. The winter storm caused disruptions to travel and one fatality due to an accident on Interstate 10 east of Junction.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was 1 to 2 inches of snow/sleet across Irion County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extended period of below normal rainfall coupled with much above normal temperatures from September through December 2025 led to the development of severe (D2) and extreme (D3) drought conditions from the U.S. Drought Monitor across much of Red River County Texas from the second week of January through the end of the month. During this time, rainfall amounts ranged from 4-7 inches, which was only around 25% of normal, resulting in considerable hardships from area agricultural producers. Pastures had become dormant early, forcing farmers to provide hay and other supplemental feed much earlier than normal, as well as water as multiple ponds had dried up. An increase in wildfires was also noted especially as lower humidities to end the year accelerated the drying of fuels. These conditions only accelerated during December as only a tenth up to a half inch of rain fell during the month throughout the county. Streamflow from area creeks and bayous also fell below to much below normal as well.

A weak cold front extended from Eastern Oklahoma into Northeast Texas during the late evening into the early morning hours of January 9th-10th, and separated a warm, moist, and unstable air mass to its east, with a slightly cooler but drier air mass to the west. A surface low developed during the evening and overnight hours along the frontal boundary, enhancing a deep southerly low level flow into East Texas and North Louisiana, with a longwave trough shifting east through the Rockies into the Southern Plains enhancing large scale forcing along and ahead of the front across the warm sector. Thus, scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms developed across this area, some of which produced locally heavy rainfall in the city of Tyler in Smith County Texas. Localized flash flooding was observed here where high water covered Loop 323 near Troup Highway and University Street, before the storms shifted east into Northwest Louisiana shortly after midnight and produced insolated instances of severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High water covered Loop 323 near Troup Highway and University Street in Tyler.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extended period of below normal rainfall coupled with much above normal temperatures from October 2025 through the first half of January 2026 led to the development of severe (D2) drought conditions from the U.S. Drought Monitor across portions of East Texas from the second week of January through the end of the month. During this time, rainfall amounts ranged from 5-8 inches, which was only 25-30% of normal, resulting in considerable hardships from area agricultural producers. Pastures had become dormant early, forcing farmers to provide hay and other supplemental feed much earlier than normal, as well as water as multiple ponds had dried up. An increase in wildfires was also noted especially as lower humidities to end the year accelerated the drying of fuels. Conditions deteriorated to extreme drought across Franklin and Titus Counties by January 22nd through the end of the month as only 1.0-1.5 inches of precipitation fell during the month. Streamflow from area creeks and bayous also fell below to much below normal as well.

A strong surge of Arctic air invaded East and Northeast Texas during late January with bitterly cold temperatures and light northerly winds. In wake of the this Arctic air arriving on the evening of January 24th, overnight low temperatures plummeted into the teens and lower 20s by the morning on January 26th and again on the 27th. Although northerly winds were relatively light throughout much of the event, they only further contributed to the dangerously cold temperatures across East and Northeast Texas from late evening on January 25th through late morning on January 27th.

A strong arctic cold front moved south into the Four State Region during the afternoon through the overnight hours on January 23rd-24th, with temperatures quickly falling from the upper 40s to mid 50s on the 23rd into the 20s and lower 30s by the early morning hours on the 24th. Meanwhile, a southwest flow aloft developed over much of the Central and Southern Plains into the Eastern United States, ahead of a large upper level trough that developed along the West Coast and Desert Southwest, and drifted slowly east through the Rockies, Plains, and Mississippi Valley before weakening. This trough tapped ample moisture from the Southern Pacific, with strong overrunning that developed atop the shallow arctic air mass which contributed to widespread periods of freezing rain and sleet across the Four State Region through the morning of the 25th. Significant ice and sleet accumulations were observed, with the bitter cold air mass lingering through the morning of the 28th, before enough thawing was observed to melt the ice off of area trees, A strong surge of Arctic air invaded East and Northeast Texas during late January with bitterly cold temperatures and light northerly winds. In wake of the this Arctic air arriving on the evening of January 24th, overnight low temperatures plummeted into the teens and lower 20s by the morning on January 26th and again on the 27th. Although northerly winds were relatively light throughout much of the event, they only further contributed to the dangerously cold temperatures across East and Northeast Texas from late evening on January 25th through late morning on January 27th.

An extended period of below normal rainfall coupled with much above normal temperatures from October 2025 through the first half of January 2026 led to the development of severe (D2) drought conditions from the U.S. Drought Monitor across portions of East Texas from the second week of January through the end of the month. During this time, rainfall amounts ranged from 5-8 inches, which was only 25-30% of normal, resulting in considerable hardships from area agricultural producers. Pastures had become dormant early, forcing farmers to provide hay and other supplemental feed much earlier than normal, as well as water as multiple ponds had dried up. An increase in wildfires was also noted especially as lower humidities to end the year accelerated the drying of fuels. Conditions deteriorated to extreme drought across Franklin and Titus Counties by January 22nd through the end of the month as only 1.0-1.5 inches of precipitation fell during the month. Streamflow from area creeks and bayous also fell below to much below normal as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought sleet/snow to much of west central Texas. Some freezing rain resulted in a light glaze of ice across the Northwest Hill Country. The snow/sleet amounts ranged from 1 to 4 inches across the northern 2/3 of the area. The winter storm caused disruptions to travel and one fatality due to an accident on Interstate 10 east of Junction.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was 2 to 4 inches of snow/sleet across Throckmorton County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant winter storm occurred across the southern, eastern, and northeastern United States during January 23-26, 2026. A strong low pressure system moved across Baja California toward the Desert Southwest and then across the southern to northeastern portions of the country during this time frame. This low pressure system was preceded by a strong arctic cold front, which dropped surface temperatures down to record breaking lows across portions of the southern United States, including the Texas and Oklahoma Panhandles. Winter impacts were felt across nearly half of the country, with around an inch of freezing rain occurring at some areas across the southeastern US, inches of sleet across portions of north Texas, and up to one to two feet of snow for the Midwest into New England. This winter storm brought mostly snow to the region, but also there was some sleet mixed in across the southern and southeastern Texas Panhandle during the onset of precipitation across the region. Two to three day snow accumulation across the area ranged from just over an inch across the northwestern Panhandles to around nine to ten inches for the southeast Texas Panhandle. Frigid temperatures were also associated with this system and wind chill values dropped to as low as -20 to -25��F in the early morning hours during this time period. Temperatures remained below freezing across most of the Panhandles from early Friday morning behind the cold front passage until midday on Monday, January 26. The combination of the dangerously cold temperatures and impactful snowfall led to the closure of multiple school districts across the region as well as businesses and local government buildings. A few locations also saw infrastructure impacts such as pipes bursting in the region. Several counties also opened warming centers for the prolonged cold weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spearman and Gruver both received an estimate two inches of snow. Overall the county observed up to two inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The only winter weather event of the season occurred January 23-25. A mix of sleet and snow fell across the region, but sleet was the predominant precipitation type. The highest sleet and snow totals were north of Interstate 20, and the highest freezing rain totals were in our far eastern counties. In addition to the wintry precipitation, bitterly cold air accompanied the system meeting Extreme Cold Warning criteria through the 27th. Unfortunately 8 deaths were reported with this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sleet and snow totals up to 2 inches were reported in Lamar County, along with reports of freezing rain accumulations up to 0.25 inches. The highest sleet, snow, and freezing rain totals were reported near Paris. Scattered trees and power lines were knocked down due to ice across Lamar County with some roads blocked and power outages, especially from Paris into northeast portions of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Arctic cold front ushered in the coldest air mass of the season to Deep South Texas and the Rio Grande Valley with widespread cold temperatures between January 25-27, 2026. Cold temperatures (wind chill) below 30F arrived during the late evening of the 24th across the ranchlands and spread to the lower Rio Grande Valley before daybreak on the 25th.  A cold afternoon set the stage for the overnight of the 25th into the morning of the 26th, as skies cleared but fresh breezes/stronger winds near the coast prevailed.  The 26th was the coldest ambient and feels-like temperature of the calendar year so far, with a freeze in all areas, and wind chill below 20F in most areas.  ||Clear skies and calm winds allowed rapid temperature drops during the evening of the 26th, with many inland locations recording their second hard freeze in as many nights early on the 27th. Locations closer to the coast saw winds veer to the northeast above the surface, precluding a second freeze/hard freeze. ||The hard freeze caused minor damage to tender vegetation and crops in Hidalgo, Willacy, and Cameron County.  Dollar values were unknown as of this writing (end of March 2026) but some estimates are included, and will likely be updated.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Brownsville/South Padre Island International Airport  (BRO) recorded minimum wind chill values at or below 30F degrees (Cold Weather Advisory Criteria) for 6 hours from 4:35-10:20am CST on Sunday January 25th. Harlingen/Valley International Airport (HRL) recorded minimum wind chill values at or below 30F degrees (Cold Weather Advisory Criteria) for 11.5 hours from 11:25pm (Jan 24th) to 10:40am CST January 25th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level system skirted across the northern combined Oklahoma and Texas Panhandles early on January 9th and brought rain with a changeover to snow. The heaviest snow produced by this system occurred in Kansas but did bring around three to six inches to eastern Cimarron County and western Texas County. Further south, across the far northwestern Texas Panhandle, areas still received one to two inches of snow from this system. This weather system was located far enough north that the rest of the Texas Panhandle was left dry.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around one inch of snow was measured four miles north-northwest of Hartley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant winter storm occurred across the southern, eastern, and northeastern United States during January 23-26, 2026. A strong low pressure system moved across Baja California toward the Desert Southwest and then across the southern to northeastern portions of the country during this time frame. This low pressure system was preceded by a strong arctic cold front, which dropped surface temperatures down to record breaking lows across portions of the southern United States, including the Texas and Oklahoma Panhandles. Winter impacts were felt across nearly half of the country, with around an inch of freezing rain occurring at some areas across the southeastern US, inches of sleet across portions of north Texas, and up to one to two feet of snow for the Midwest into New England. This winter storm brought mostly snow to the region, but also there was some sleet mixed in across the southern and southeastern Texas Panhandle during the onset of precipitation across the region. Two to three day snow accumulation across the area ranged from just over an inch across the northwestern Panhandles to around nine to ten inches for the southeast Texas Panhandle. Frigid temperatures were also associated with this system and wind chill values dropped to as low as -20 to -25��F in the early morning hours during this time period. Temperatures remained below freezing across most of the Panhandles from early Friday morning behind the cold front passage until midday on Monday, January 26. The combination of the dangerously cold temperatures and impactful snowfall led to the closure of multiple school districts across the region as well as businesses and local government buildings. A few locations also saw infrastructure impacts such as pipes bursting in the region. Several counties also opened warming centers for the prolonged cold weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This system brought 5 to almost 10 of snow to Wheeler County. The heaviest snow fell across the southern and eastern parts of the county. Reports of 9 inches came in from Shamrock and 9.8 inches 9 miles ESE of Shamrock. Mobeetie received 8 inches of snow and Allison was estimated to have received 6 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of winter weather affected South Texas during the final week of January 2026, beginning with the arrival of a strong Arctic front early on January 24. The front surged southward faster than initially anticipated, producing a rapid temperature drop across the region and widespread rain behind the boundary. As colder air deepened overnight, precipitation continued while temperatures approached or fell below freezing across parts of the region, creating a narrow window for freezing rain and drizzle late January 24 into the morning of January 25. The greatest risk for ice accumulation occurred across the Victoria Crossroads, where light ice totals of up to around a tenth of an inch were possible. Elsewhere, the combination of lingering precipitation and wet roadways raised concerns for patchy black ice, particularly on bridges and overpasses as temperatures briefly dipped to freezing.||Once the precipitation ended on January 25, the primary hazard transitioned to extreme cold as a deep Arctic air mass settled over South Texas. Temperatures struggled to rise above the 40s during the day, while overnight lows dropped into the teens across northern inland areas and the lower to mid-20s closer to the coast. Persistent northerly winds produced dangerously low wind chills, with values falling into the single digits in some inland locations and the teens elsewhere. Extreme Cold Warnings and Freeze Warnings were issued across the region as the prolonged cold posed risks to infrastructure, agriculture, and vulnerable populations. Clearer skies and lighter winds early in the week allowed efficient radiational cooling, reinforcing the cold conditions through January 27 before gradual moderation began.||Although temperatures slowly recovered into the 50s and 60s by midweek, the pattern remained active with additional frontal passages. A brief warmup ahead of another cold front late in the week pushed temperatures into the 70s before colder air returned by January 30. This secondary surge brought another round of below-freezing temperatures and widespread cold weather headlines, particularly across inland counties where lows fell into the 20s and wind chills dropped into the teens. While precipitation with this second front remained limited, the extended period of repeated freezes and persistent cold made this late-January event a notable winter episode for South Texas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme Cold/Wind Chill: KVCT reported wind chills at or below 15 degF for at least 2 hours, which is our local Extreme Cold Warning criteria.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Arctic cold front ushered in the coldest air mass of the season to Deep South Texas and the Rio Grande Valley with widespread cold temperatures between January 25-27, 2026. Cold temperatures (wind chill) below 30F arrived during the late evening of the 24th across the ranchlands and spread to the lower Rio Grande Valley before daybreak on the 25th.  A cold afternoon set the stage for the overnight of the 25th into the morning of the 26th, as skies cleared but fresh breezes/stronger winds near the coast prevailed.  The 26th was the coldest ambient and feels-like temperature of the calendar year so far, with a freeze in all areas, and wind chill below 20F in most areas.  ||Clear skies and calm winds allowed rapid temperature drops during the evening of the 26th, with many inland locations recording their second hard freeze in as many nights early on the 27th. Locations closer to the coast saw winds veer to the northeast above the surface, precluding a second freeze/hard freeze. ||The hard freeze caused minor damage to tender vegetation and crops in Hidalgo, Willacy, and Cameron County. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper-level low moved across northern Mexico bringing a strong cold front through South-Central Texas. This resulted in very cold air in the boundary layer with a layer of above freezing air aloft which produced sleet and freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind chill temperature dropped to 8 degrees in Giddings.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level system skirted across the northern combined Oklahoma and Texas Panhandles early on January 9th and brought rain with a changeover to snow. The heaviest snow produced by this system occurred in Kansas but did bring around three to six inches to eastern Cimarron County and western Texas County. Further south, across the far northwestern Texas Panhandle, areas still received one to two inches of snow from this system. This weather system was located far enough north that the rest of the Texas Panhandle was left dry.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Parts of the county, mainly in the north like around Gruver received around one inch of snow from this system.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through the early morning hours on January 16th and brought gusty north winds. Though most areas observed wind gusts in the 45 to 57 mph range, there were a couple of instances where a 58 mph or higher wind gust was briefly measured.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple wind gusts surpassed 60 mph. The first high wind gust of 63 mph occurred at 0155CST. The last high wind gust occurred at 0227CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through the early morning hours on January 16th and brought gusty north winds. Though most areas observed wind gusts in the 45 to 57 mph range, there were a couple of instances where a 58 mph or higher wind gust was briefly measured.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The mesonet site located three miles southeast of Dumas measured a single 60mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the overnight hours of January 20th into the 21st, a cold front with a strong pressure gradient brought gusty winds into the combined Oklahoma and Texas Panhandles. While most places in the Panhandles saw northerly winds gusting up to 45 to 55 mph, there were isolated instances of measured gusts in the 58 to 66 mph range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS located at the Dalhart Municipal Airport recorded the first high wind gust around 1250CST and the last high wind gust occurred at 1255CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The only winter weather event of the season occurred January 23-25. A mix of sleet and snow fell across the region, but sleet was the predominant precipitation type. The highest sleet and snow totals were north of Interstate 20, and the highest freezing rain totals were in our far eastern counties. In addition to the wintry precipitation, bitterly cold air accompanied the system meeting Extreme Cold Warning criteria through the 27th. Unfortunately 8 deaths were reported with this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sleet and snow totals between 1.5 and 5 inches were reported across Parker County. The highest sleet and snow totals were reported just southwest of Reno. The Emergency Manager reported a roof collapse at GNJ Pickleball in Willow Park. The estimated damage to the metal warehouse facility was $2 million.

Locations closer to the coast saw winds veer to the northeast above the surface, precluding a second freeze/hard freeze. ||The hard freeze caused minor damage to tender vegetation and crops in Hidalgo, Willacy, and Cameron County.  Dollar values were unknown as of this writing (end of March 2026) but some estimates are included, and will likely be updated.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme Cold Warning Criteria (Wind chills at or below 20F) was reached at Harlingen/Valley International Airport for 6.25 hours from 3:00-10:00am CST Monday January 26th.  By proxy with other locations in northeast Hidalgo County and southeast Kenedy County, it is assumed wind chill fell to or below 20F for around 7 hours.  Estimated minimum wind chill in Willacy County was around 15F.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observations from several locations in the northern half of inland Cameron County reached the freezing level (32F or lower) early on the 27th.  This included Harlingen, San Benito, Santa Rosa, and La Feria.  Harlingen/Valley International Airport fell to 30F, while mesonet platforms in Santa Rosa and northwest Harlingen fell to 28F. Durations were much shorter than on the 26th, as low level winds veered to the northeast.  Duration of freezing temperatures (32F or lower) was around 2 hours.  Locations in inland southern Cameron, including Brownsville, remained above freezing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: At the start of the new year, Severe (D2) Drought was sited across much of the Northern Ranchlands and the Upper valley including the Northwestern section of Kenedy County, southeastern Brooks, northwestern Jim Hogg, eastern Zapata, and much of Starr County. Meanwhile, Extreme (D3) Drought was over much of Brooks and Jim Hogg counties, northwestern Starr county, and extreme northwestern Kenedy County at the start of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: According to the U.S. Drought Monitor, D2 Severe Drought to D3 Extreme Drought was seen over Brooks County at the start of the month of January, with D3 Drought encompassing the northwestern half of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the overnight hours of January 20th into the 21st, a cold front with a strong pressure gradient brought gusty winds into the combined Oklahoma and Texas Panhandles. While most places in the Panhandles saw northerly winds gusting up to 45 to 55 mph, there were isolated instances of measured gusts in the 58 to 66 mph range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The mesonet site located two miles north of Stinnett measured 61mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level system skirted across the northern combined Oklahoma and Texas Panhandles early on January 9th and brought rain with a changeover to snow. The heaviest snow produced by this system occurred in Kansas but did bring around three to six inches to eastern Cimarron County and western Texas County. Further south, across the far northwestern Texas Panhandle, areas still received one to two inches of snow from this system. This weather system was located far enough north that the rest of the Texas Panhandle was left dry.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to two inches of snow fell across the northern two thirds of the county, with Texline stations observing around 1.5 to 2 inches. Further south totals fell off significantly to less than one inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The only winter weather event of the season occurred January 23-25. A mix of sleet and snow fell across the region, but sleet was the predominant precipitation type. The highest sleet and snow totals were north of Interstate 20, and the highest freezing rain totals were in our far eastern counties. In addition to the wintry precipitation, bitterly cold air accompanied the system meeting Extreme Cold Warning criteria through the 27th. Unfortunately 8 deaths were reported with this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sleet and snow totals between 0.1 to 1.8 inches were reported across Van Zandt County, along with reports of freezing rain accumulations up to 0.1 inches. The highest sleet and snow totals were reported in Wills Point. Several wrecks and 18 wheeler slide offs were reported on I-20 across Van Zandt County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought in freezing temperatures to the Northern Ranchlands from northern Jim Hogg to Kenedy County on January 18-19. The front followed a prolonged stretch of unseasonably warm weather through the 9th, then a period of near-average conditions from the 10th-17th. On January 19th, the combination of the cold airmass and strong radiational cooling resulted in freezing temperatures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hebbronville recorded freezing temperatures (32F degrees or lower) for 2 hours from 5:35-7:35am CST on January 18th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant winter storm occurred across the southern, eastern, and northeastern United States during January 23-26, 2026. A strong low pressure system moved across Baja California toward the Desert Southwest and then across the southern to northeastern portions of the country during this time frame. This low pressure system was preceded by a strong arctic cold front, which dropped surface temperatures down to record breaking lows across portions of the southern United States, including the Texas and Oklahoma Panhandles. Winter impacts were felt across nearly half of the country, with around an inch of freezing rain occurring at some areas across the southeastern US, inches of sleet across portions of north Texas, and up to one to two feet of snow for the Midwest into New England. This winter storm brought mostly snow to the region, but also there was some sleet mixed in across the southern and southeastern Texas Panhandle during the onset of precipitation across the region. Two to three day snow accumulation across the area ranged from just over an inch across the northwestern Panhandles to around nine to ten inches for the southeast Texas Panhandle. Frigid temperatures were also associated with this system and wind chill values dropped to as low as -20 to -25��F in the early morning hours during this time period. Temperatures remained below freezing across most of the Panhandles from early Friday morning behind the cold front passage until midday on Monday, January 26. The combination of the dangerously cold temperatures and impactful snowfall led to the closure of multiple school districts across the region as well as businesses and local government buildings. A few locations also saw infrastructure impacts such as pipes bursting in the region. Several counties also opened warming centers for the prolonged cold weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A sharp gradient of snow occurred from northwest to southeast across the county. The lowest snowfall amounts of around one to two inches occurred in the northwest while amounts around three inches occurred in the southeast. Stinnett measured one inch of snow, 1.6 inches was measured just west of Fritch and around 2.9 to 3 inches of snow occurred around Borger.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Arctic cold front ushered in the coldest air mass of the season to Deep South Texas and the Rio Grande Valley with widespread cold temperatures between January 25-27, 2026. Cold temperatures (wind chill) below 30F arrived during the late evening of the 24th across the ranchlands and spread to the lower Rio Grande Valley before daybreak on the 25th.  A cold afternoon set the stage for the overnight of the 25th into the morning of the 26th, as skies cleared but fresh breezes/stronger winds near the coast prevailed.  The 26th was the coldest ambient and feels-like temperature of the calendar year so far, with a freeze in all areas, and wind chill below 20F in most areas.  ||Clear skies and calm winds allowed rapid temperature drops during the evening of the 26th, with many inland locations recording their second hard freeze in as many nights early on the 27th. Locations closer to the coast saw winds veer to the northeast above the surface, precluding a second freeze/hard freeze. ||The hard freeze caused minor damage to tender vegetation and crops in Hidalgo, Willacy, and Cameron County.  Dollar values were unknown as of this writing (end of March 2026) but some estimates are included, and will likely be updated.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Brownsville/South Padre Island International Airport (BRO) recorded freezing temperatures (32F degrees or lower) for 4.5 hours from 4:15-8:55am CST on Monday January 26th. Minimum temperatures were in the mid to upper 20s.||An unknown amount of minor damage was likely to winter crops, mainly due to the sharp temperature change from prior to the freeze until after it.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: At the start of the new year, Severe (D2) Drought was sited across much of the Northern Ranchlands and the Upper valley including the Northwestern section of Kenedy County, southeastern Brooks, northwestern Jim Hogg, eastern Zapata, and much of Starr County. Meanwhile, Extreme (D3) Drought was over much of Brooks and Jim Hogg counties, northwestern Starr county, and extreme northwestern Kenedy County at the start of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: According to the U.S. Drought Monitor, D2 Severe Drought was observed across the eastern half of Zapata County with D3 Extreme Drought observed over the extreme southeastern section of Zapata County at the start of the month of January, continuing through the 12th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant winter storm occurred across the southern, eastern, and northeastern United States during January 23-26, 2026. A strong low pressure system moved across Baja California toward the Desert Southwest and then across the southern to northeastern portions of the country during this time frame. This low pressure system was preceded by a strong arctic cold front, which dropped surface temperatures down to record breaking lows across portions of the southern United States, including the Texas and Oklahoma Panhandles. Winter impacts were felt across nearly half of the country, with around an inch of freezing rain occurring at some areas across the southeastern US, inches of sleet across portions of north Texas, and up to one to two feet of snow for the Midwest into New England. This winter storm brought mostly snow to the region, but also there was some sleet mixed in across the southern and southeastern Texas Panhandle during the onset of precipitation across the region. Two to three day snow accumulation across the area ranged from just over an inch across the northwestern Panhandles to around nine to ten inches for the southeast Texas Panhandle. Frigid temperatures were also associated with this system and wind chill values dropped to as low as -20 to -25��F in the early morning hours during this time period. Temperatures remained below freezing across most of the Panhandles from early Friday morning behind the cold front passage until midday on Monday, January 26. The combination of the dangerously cold temperatures and impactful snowfall led to the closure of multiple school districts across the region as well as businesses and local government buildings. A few locations also saw infrastructure impacts such as pipes bursting in the region. Several counties also opened warming centers for the prolonged cold weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This system dropped 2 to 4 of snow in the northern two thirds of the county from Canyon to Amarillo. While it is possible there was up to 5 inches in the south, closest to the heavier swath of snow, there were no measurements confirmed. 2.4 to 3 inches of snow fell around Canyon and Palo Duro Canyon. The south and southwest side of Amarillo measured 2 to 3 inches.

Coverage of Extreme Drought increased to more than 70% of these areas by the 13th, which continued through month's end.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: By mid month, Extreme (D3) Drought conditions expanded southeastward to encompass much of Brooks County with the exception of the southeastern part of the county, which remained under Severe (D2) Drought. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Across South Texas and the Coastal Bend, persistent dry conditions through late January led to worsening drought and critically low soil moisture, limiting agricultural progress and stressing rangeland. Periods of unseasonably warm weather were followed by hard freezes and a winter storm, though precipitation was generally limited, with only modest rainfall of up to around an inch in parts of the Coastal Bend providing minor short-term improvement to soil moisture. Producers largely completed field preparation but delayed spring planting due to insufficient moisture for germination. Winter pastures and forage growth struggled under the dry conditions, resulting in poor to fair range conditions and increased reliance on hay and supplemental feed for livestock. While citrus and cool-season vegetable harvests continued, drought stress remained a concern for crop development and pasture recovery, and additional rainfall will be needed to improve soil moisture and support the upcoming planting season.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Continued D2 drought.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The passage of a very subtle reinforcing front and high pressure settling over the region and gradually progressing eastward through the night, locked in light, variable, but mostly offshore winds for a cool, dry overnight into Sunday morning. This set us up for a chilly night, with light freezes experienced across much of the northern CWA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: KBEA reported temperatures of 32 degF or less for a least 2 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant winter storm occurred across the southern, eastern, and northeastern United States during January 23-26, 2026. A strong low pressure system moved across Baja California toward the Desert Southwest and then across the southern to northeastern portions of the country during this time frame. This low pressure system was preceded by a strong arctic cold front, which dropped surface temperatures down to record breaking lows across portions of the southern United States, including the Texas and Oklahoma Panhandles. Winter impacts were felt across nearly half of the country, with around an inch of freezing rain occurring at some areas across the southeastern US, inches of sleet across portions of north Texas, and up to one to two feet of snow for the Midwest into New England. This winter storm brought mostly snow to the region, but also there was some sleet mixed in across the southern and southeastern Texas Panhandle during the onset of precipitation across the region. Two to three day snow accumulation across the area ranged from just over an inch across the northwestern Panhandles to around nine to ten inches for the southeast Texas Panhandle. Frigid temperatures were also associated with this system and wind chill values dropped to as low as -20 to -25��F in the early morning hours during this time period. Temperatures remained below freezing across most of the Panhandles from early Friday morning behind the cold front passage until midday on Monday, January 26. The combination of the dangerously cold temperatures and impactful snowfall led to the closure of multiple school districts across the region as well as businesses and local government buildings. A few locations also saw infrastructure impacts such as pipes bursting in the region. Several counties also opened warming centers for the prolonged cold weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow measured around one inch in Stratford and for much of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The only winter weather event of the season occurred January 23-25. A mix of sleet and snow fell across the region, but sleet was the predominant precipitation type. The highest sleet and snow totals were north of Interstate 20, and the highest freezing rain totals were in our far eastern counties. In addition to the wintry precipitation, bitterly cold air accompanied the system meeting Extreme Cold Warning criteria through the 27th. Unfortunately 8 deaths were reported with this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observation sites in Falls County recorded wind chills at or below 5 degrees on the 25th of January. The lowest wind chill of 3 was recorded at a SCAN observation north of Marlin near the county line the morning of January 25th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Little to no rainfall and a mixture of heat followed by cold and generally dry weather, including a hard freeze toward the end of January combined to steadily worsen drought conditions across northern Hidalgo, Jim Hogg, Brooks, Starr, and Zapata County.  Coverage of Extreme Drought increased to more than 70% of these areas by the 13th, which continued through month's end.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: By mid month, Severe (D2) Drought expanded slightly eastward to include nearly all of Northern Hidalgo County with the exception of the extreme southeastern part. The extreme northwestern part of Northern Hidalgo County saw Extreme (D3) Drought develop by mid month. These conditions remained in place through the remainder of the month.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: By mid January, Extreme (D3) Drought encompassed all of Jim Hogg County with D3 Drought persisting through the remainder of the month.  Ranchers began to report difficulty maintaining cattle herds and needing to haul water to the ranch to sustain them.  Hay availability was becoming limited.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Arctic cold front ushered in the coldest air mass of the season to Deep South Texas and the Rio Grande Valley with widespread cold temperatures between January 25-27, 2026. Cold temperatures (wind chill) below 30F arrived during the late evening of the 24th across the ranchlands and spread to the lower Rio Grande Valley before daybreak on the 25th.  A cold afternoon set the stage for the overnight of the 25th into the morning of the 26th, as skies cleared but fresh breezes/stronger winds near the coast prevailed.  The 26th was the coldest ambient and feels-like temperature of the calendar year so far, with a freeze in all areas, and wind chill below 20F in most areas.  ||Clear skies and calm winds allowed rapid temperature drops during the evening of the 26th, with many inland locations recording their second hard freeze in as many nights early on the 27th. Locations closer to the coast saw winds veer to the northeast above the surface, precluding a second freeze/hard freeze. ||The hard freeze caused minor damage to tender vegetation and crops in Hidalgo, Willacy, and Cameron County.  Dollar values were unknown as of this writing (end of March 2026) but some estimates are included, and will likely be updated.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Realitos, TX recorded minimum wind chill values at or below 30F degrees (Cold Weather Advisory Criteria) for nearly 12 hours from 12:24am  and 12:24 pm CST on Sunday January 25th. Rincon del San Jose (southeast Kenedy) reached 27F by midnight; interpolating between the sites indicated that Willacy Island reached 30F around or just after midnight on the 25th.

Minimum temperatures were between 23 and 25F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of winter weather affected South Texas during the final week of January 2026, beginning with the arrival of a strong Arctic front early on January 24. The front surged southward faster than initially anticipated, producing a rapid temperature drop across the region and widespread rain behind the boundary. As colder air deepened overnight, precipitation continued while temperatures approached or fell below freezing across parts of the region, creating a narrow window for freezing rain and drizzle late January 24 into the morning of January 25. The greatest risk for ice accumulation occurred across the Victoria Crossroads, where light ice totals of up to around a tenth of an inch were possible. Elsewhere, the combination of lingering precipitation and wet roadways raised concerns for patchy black ice, particularly on bridges and overpasses as temperatures briefly dipped to freezing.||Once the precipitation ended on January 25, the primary hazard transitioned to extreme cold as a deep Arctic air mass settled over South Texas. Temperatures struggled to rise above the 40s during the day, while overnight lows dropped into the teens across northern inland areas and the lower to mid-20s closer to the coast. Persistent northerly winds produced dangerously low wind chills, with values falling into the single digits in some inland locations and the teens elsewhere. Extreme Cold Warnings and Freeze Warnings were issued across the region as the prolonged cold posed risks to infrastructure, agriculture, and vulnerable populations. Clearer skies and lighter winds early in the week allowed efficient radiational cooling, reinforcing the cold conditions through January 27 before gradual moderation began.||Although temperatures slowly recovered into the 50s and 60s by midweek, the pattern remained active with additional frontal passages. A brief warmup ahead of another cold front late in the week pushed temperatures into the 70s before colder air returned by January 30. This secondary surge brought another round of below-freezing temperatures and widespread cold weather headlines, particularly across inland counties where lows fell into the 20s and wind chills dropped into the teens. While precipitation with this second front remained limited, the extended period of repeated freezes and persistent cold made this late-January event a notable winter episode for South Texas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: KCOT reported wind chills at or below 25 degF for at least 2 hours, which is our local Cold Weather Advisory criteria.

The front surged southward faster than initially anticipated, producing a rapid temperature drop across the region and widespread rain behind the boundary. As colder air deepened overnight, precipitation continued while temperatures approached or fell below freezing across parts of the region, creating a narrow window for freezing rain and drizzle late January 24 into the morning of January 25. The greatest risk for ice accumulation occurred across the Victoria Crossroads, where light ice totals of up to around a tenth of an inch were possible. Elsewhere, the combination of lingering precipitation and wet roadways raised concerns for patchy black ice, particularly on bridges and overpasses as temperatures briefly dipped to freezing.||Once the precipitation ended on January 25, the primary hazard transitioned to extreme cold as a deep Arctic air mass settled over South Texas. Temperatures struggled to rise above the 40s during the day, while overnight lows dropped into the teens across northern inland areas and the lower to mid-20s closer to the coast. Persistent northerly winds produced dangerously low wind chills, with values falling into the single digits in some inland locations and the teens elsewhere. Extreme Cold Warnings and Freeze Warnings were issued across the region as the prolonged cold posed risks to infrastructure, agriculture, and vulnerable populations. Clearer skies and lighter winds early in the week allowed efficient radiational cooling, reinforcing the cold conditions through January 27 before gradual moderation began.||Although temperatures slowly recovered into the 50s and 60s by midweek, the pattern remained active with additional frontal passages. A brief warmup ahead of another cold front late in the week pushed temperatures into the 70s before colder air returned by January 30. This secondary surge brought another round of below-freezing temperatures and widespread cold weather headlines, particularly across inland counties where lows fell into the 20s and wind chills dropped into the teens. While precipitation with this second front remained limited, the extended period of repeated freezes and persistent cold made this late-January event a notable winter episode for South Texas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: KRAS reported wind chills at or below 25 degF for at least 2 hours, which is our local Cold Weather Advisory criteria.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The only winter weather event of the season occurred January 23-25. A mix of sleet and snow fell across the region, but sleet was the predominant precipitation type. The highest sleet and snow totals were north of Interstate 20, and the highest freezing rain totals were in our far eastern counties. In addition to the wintry precipitation, bitterly cold air accompanied the system meeting Extreme Cold Warning criteria through the 27th. Unfortunately 8 deaths were reported with this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills dropped below 0 degrees across the county between Saturday evening and Tuesday morning, January 24th through 27th. The lowest wind chill of 0 was recorded at KPRX the morning of January 25th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Arctic cold front ushered in the coldest air mass of the season to Deep South Texas and the Rio Grande Valley with widespread cold temperatures between January 25-27, 2026. Cold temperatures (wind chill) below 30F arrived during the late evening of the 24th across the ranchlands and spread to the lower Rio Grande Valley before daybreak on the 25th.  A cold afternoon set the stage for the overnight of the 25th into the morning of the 26th, as skies cleared but fresh breezes/stronger winds near the coast prevailed.  The 26th was the coldest ambient and feels-like temperature of the calendar year so far, with a freeze in all areas, and wind chill below 20F in most areas.  ||Clear skies and calm winds allowed rapid temperature drops during the evening of the 26th, with many inland locations recording their second hard freeze in as many nights early on the 27th. Locations closer to the coast saw winds veer to the northeast above the surface, precluding a second freeze/hard freeze. ||The hard freeze caused minor damage to tender vegetation and crops in Hidalgo, Willacy, and Cameron County.  Dollar values were unknown as of this writing (end of March 2026) but some estimates are included, and will likely be updated.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observations from the Sarita mesonet site, the Yturria Ranch near the Willacy/Kenedy line, Falfurrias (northeast Brooks), and the minimum temperature in Armstrong indicated nearly 12 hours of freezing (32F or lower) temperatures in inland Willacy, with a hard freeze (27F or lower) between 1030 PM and 8 AM as radiational cooling maximized. Minimum temperatures were between 18F and 22F, the coldest morning of the event.

The highest sleet and snow totals were north of Interstate 20, and the highest freezing rain totals were in our far eastern counties. In addition to the wintry precipitation, bitterly cold air accompanied the system meeting Extreme Cold Warning criteria through the 27th. Unfortunately 8 deaths were reported with this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills dropped below 5 degrees across the county between Saturday evening and Tuesday morning, January 24th through 27th. The lowest wind chill of 2 was recorded at a UPR observation site just north of Wortham the morning of January 25th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of winter weather affected South Texas during the final week of January 2026, beginning with the arrival of a strong Arctic front early on January 24. The front surged southward faster than initially anticipated, producing a rapid temperature drop across the region and widespread rain behind the boundary. As colder air deepened overnight, precipitation continued while temperatures approached or fell below freezing across parts of the region, creating a narrow window for freezing rain and drizzle late January 24 into the morning of January 25. The greatest risk for ice accumulation occurred across the Victoria Crossroads, where light ice totals of up to around a tenth of an inch were possible. Elsewhere, the combination of lingering precipitation and wet roadways raised concerns for patchy black ice, particularly on bridges and overpasses as temperatures briefly dipped to freezing.||Once the precipitation ended on January 25, the primary hazard transitioned to extreme cold as a deep Arctic air mass settled over South Texas. Temperatures struggled to rise above the 40s during the day, while overnight lows dropped into the teens across northern inland areas and the lower to mid-20s closer to the coast. Persistent northerly winds produced dangerously low wind chills, with values falling into the single digits in some inland locations and the teens elsewhere. Extreme Cold Warnings and Freeze Warnings were issued across the region as the prolonged cold posed risks to infrastructure, agriculture, and vulnerable populations. Clearer skies and lighter winds early in the week allowed efficient radiational cooling, reinforcing the cold conditions through January 27 before gradual moderation began.||Although temperatures slowly recovered into the 50s and 60s by midweek, the pattern remained active with additional frontal passages. A brief warmup ahead of another cold front late in the week pushed temperatures into the 70s before colder air returned by January 30. This secondary surge brought another round of below-freezing temperatures and widespread cold weather headlines, particularly across inland counties where lows fell into the 20s and wind chills dropped into the teens. While precipitation with this second front remained limited, the extended period of repeated freezes and persistent cold made this late-January event a notable winter episode for South Texas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: KVCT reported wind chills at or below 25 degF for at least 2 hours, which is our local Cold Weather Advisory criteria.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds with a storm near Olney in Young Country produced scattered wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reported a southwest to northeast swath of damage near Olney. Multiple power poles were snapped, roofs and carports were damaged, a short concrete block dividing wall was destroyed, and a silo tipped over onto a truck. Additionally, a resident's bee hives were blown away. Damage was consistent with 80 to 95 mph winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant winter storm occurred across the southern, eastern, and northeastern United States during January 23-26, 2026. A strong low pressure system moved across Baja California toward the Desert Southwest and then across the southern to northeastern portions of the country during this time frame. This low pressure system was preceded by a strong arctic cold front, which dropped surface temperatures down to record breaking lows across portions of the southern United States, including the Texas and Oklahoma Panhandles. Winter impacts were felt across nearly half of the country, with around an inch of freezing rain occurring at some areas across the southeastern US, inches of sleet across portions of north Texas, and up to one to two feet of snow for the Midwest into New England. This winter storm brought mostly snow to the region, but also there was some sleet mixed in across the southern and southeastern Texas Panhandle during the onset of precipitation across the region. Two to three day snow accumulation across the area ranged from just over an inch across the northwestern Panhandles to around nine to ten inches for the southeast Texas Panhandle. Frigid temperatures were also associated with this system and wind chill values dropped to as low as -20 to -25��F in the early morning hours during this time period. Temperatures remained below freezing across most of the Panhandles from early Friday morning behind the cold front passage until midday on Monday, January 26. The combination of the dangerously cold temperatures and impactful snowfall led to the closure of multiple school districts across the region as well as businesses and local government buildings. A few locations also saw infrastructure impacts such as pipes bursting in the region. Several counties also opened warming centers for the prolonged cold weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Donley County observed the heaviest band of snow, which occurred mainly from the afternoon of January 24th through the early morning hours of January 25th. Much of the county received 8 to 11 inches of snow. The northwest, especially where I-40 dips in and out of the county received closer to the four to six inch range.  Clarendon measured 9.5 to 10.2 inches, Howardwick measured 9 inches, 7 miles east-northeast of Hedley estimated 7 inches of snow and 8 inches of snow occurred two miles south-southeast of Greenbelt Lake. Some sleet occurred during the onset of precipitation on the afternoon and early evening of January 24th.
